title: A STUDY OF POSTMODERNIST OUTLOOK AND TREATMENT OF POLITICS, MYTHOLOGY AND RELIGION IN THE NOVELS OF SHASHI THAROOR
author: M. VENKATESAN

key_claims: Identifies the work as a thesis submitted to Bharathidasan University for the award of a Doctor of Philosophy in English
Establishes the primary focus on Shashi Tharoor’s treatment of politics, mythology, and religion through a postmodernist lens
chapters.limitations: Contains no argumentative content; serves as administrative front matter

key_claims: Certifies the thesis as a bonafide record of original research work conducted by the candidate under institutional supervision
Confirms that the work has not been previously submitted for any other degree or diploma
chapters.limitations: Legal/administrative document; does not support literary arguments

key_claims: Argues that Shashi Tharoor's novels serve as a comprehensive ground for studying Indian myths, politics, and religion in various dimensions
Defines postmodernism as an umbrella term that breaks the tenets of modernistic ideology to pull marginalized perspectives to the center
Claims that Tharoor uses religious clashes as a backdrop to question blind beliefs in society through postmodern tools
chapters.limitations: Provides a high-level overview without the granular textual analysis found in the body chapters

key_claims: Claims that Indian English literature has evolved from a colonial offshoot into a prestigious global phenomenon that gives voice to the average citizen
Argues that postmodernism creates a state of 'virtual reality' where absolute truths are replaced by relative, fragmented perspectives
Proposes that Tharoor's 'faction'—the blurring of fact and fiction—reinvents history through parodic and symbolic redactions of ancient epics
chapters.limitations: Cannot support specific close-readings of novel plots as it remains at the theoretical and biographical level

key_claims: Argues that political fiction in the post-war era explores the impact of power politics on the angst of post-atomic humanity
Claims that Tharoor’s The Great Indian Novel recasts the history of Indian democracy as an allegorical struggle between individuals for personal gain
Posits that Show Business satirizes the 'reel' world of Bollywood as a mirror for the corrupt 'real' world of Indian parliamentary politics
Maintains that Riot explores the institutionalized system of riots as a political strategy used to orchestrate violence and exert social control
chapters.limitations: Does not address the spiritual or mythological dimensions of the texts in depth

key_claims: Claims that Tharoor demythifies the Mahabharata by stripping away heroic grandeur to represent the 'pedestrian politics' of 20th-century India
Argues that the use of archetypal patterns helps the Indian reader find connections between ancient tradition and modern reality
Proposes that the polyphonic narrative in Riot demonstrates the 'unknowability of truth' by presenting contradictory versions of the same historical event
chapters.limitations: Focuses on mythological structure; less emphasis on contemporary sociological impacts of policy

key_claims: Argues that religion in India is a 'way of life' that serves as the linking thread between social identity and political mobilization
Claims that the Ayodhya dispute is a manifestation of 'identity crisis' where politicians manipulate faith for electoral vote banks
Posits that Tharoor’s work champions a 'composite culture' where every Indian is a minority within a minority, necessitating a pluralist state
chapters.limitations: Does not provide detailed analysis of narrative techniques, which are reserved for the following chapter

key_claims: Argues that Tharoor employs 'historiographical metafiction' to show that writing history is itself a fictional act mediated by language
Claims that the use of 'pastiche' and 'parody' allows Tharoor to engage in a dialogic relationship with both the reader and previous literary canons
Proposes that non-linear 'text time' in Riot forces the reader to participate in the construction of meaning from fragmented documents
chapters.limitations: Focuses on form over the political or religious content discussed in prior chapters

key_claims: Concludes that Tharoor’s fiction serves as an indictment of the 'self-oriented' polity that emerged following the partition of India
Argues that the Emergency represents a 'molestation of democracy' that echoes the mythological disrobing of Draupadi
Maintains that the only way to preserve Indian secularism is to move beyond communal symbols toward a democracy that meets material needs
chapters.limitations: Synthesizes previous findings; does not introduce new primary text analysis
